Close air support missions in complex operational environments risk civilian casualties despite precision-guided platforms, underscoring ongoing challenges in target verification procedures.

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Do Airlines Make Money From Economy Seats?

This heavily depends on the carrier and its operations.

Economy seat profitability varies significantly by airline based on cost structure, load factors, and ancillary revenue strategies rather than base fares alone.

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Alaska Airlines Pilots Ask For Police “To Meet The Airplane” After Passenger Vapes Onboard

An Alaska Airlines flight from Seattle to Sacramento had to request police to meet the aircraft at the gate after a passenger allegedly vaped.

Crew requests for police intervention over in-flight vaping incidents signal escalating safety and security concerns that may prompt stricter enforcement protocols across carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Regulatory Mar 29, 2026

SMS Declaration of Compliance

What the SMS Declaration of Compliance means for operators and what the FAA expects.

SMS Declaration of Compliance demonstrates an operator's commitment to proactive safety management systems, allowing the FAA to shift from reactive oversight to validating organizational safety culture maturity.

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

I’m A Flight Attendant: This Is Why Boarding Is The Most Stressful Part Of The Job

A lot of responsibility in a short time.

Flight attendants face compressed timelines during boarding that create cascading safety and service challenges, highlighting why airlines should reconsider accelerated boarding procedures.

Military Mar 29, 2026

6 Military Aircraft With The Most Complex Avionics

The flying supercomputers of 2026.

Modern military avionics integration increasingly demands pilot training protocols similar to software development, fundamentally changing how operators master multi-system interdependencies.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 29, 2026

Why Do Airlines Keep Older Aircraft In Service Longer Than Expected?

Older aircraft come with higher operating costs, but have unique capabilities and have already been paid off by their operator.

Fully depreciated aircraft generate positive cash flow despite maintenance costs, making them economically rational to operate until replacement aircraft arrive during supply chain constraints.

See Simple Flying →

PAI
Regulatory Mar 29, 2026

The Evolution of Aviation SMS

From the Wright brothers to the 2024 FAA rule — how aviation safety evolved from reactive investigation to proactive risk management.

Modern SMS frameworks shift aviation from investigating accidents after they occur to systematically preventing hazards before they materialize.

JetBlue Passenger With Highest Mosaic Status Caught Performing Inappropriate Act Inflight

The ACARS message sent during JetBlue Flight 2858 revealed a shocking act taking place in the cabin, involving a passenger in Mint.

Cabin crew monitoring systems can detect behavioral incidents in real-time, creating accountability mechanisms that may deter misconduct regardless of passenger status or fare class.

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

After 20 Years, The Airbus A380 Still Shapes Airport Design

A closer look at some of the upgrades that airports had to make in order to accommodate the superjumbo.

The A380's infrastructure requirements have created permanent airport modifications that now limit fleet flexibility and complicate future aircraft planning for carriers.

See Simple Flying →

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

5 Airlines With The Largest Boeing 787 Fleets In 2026

The aircraft is becoming the backbone of many airlines' fleets.

The 787's dominance in long-haul fleets signals a structural shift toward fuel-efficient wide-body aircraft that reshape route profitability and competitive positioning.

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Flying in the Unforgiving Wire Environment (The Rotorcraft Collective)

Many pilots mistakenly believe they will have sufficient reaction time if they encounter power lines and other wires. Yet wire strikes have been among the top three causes of fatal helicopter...

Wire strike fatalities persist despite pilot overconfidence in reaction time, requiring enhanced detection systems and terrain awareness training for rotorcraft operations.

Commercial Mar 28, 2026

Just 222 Seats: United Airlines' 4 Ultra-Premium Boeing 787 Routes In 2026

The new layout will initially be deployed domestically, with its international rollout coming next month.

United's ultra-premium 787 retrofit prioritizes high-yield domestic routes first, signaling a strategic shift toward maximizing per-seat revenue on transcontinental flights before expanding internationally.

These facilities are continuing to expand.

Regional capacity constraints are being addressed through coordinated infrastructure investments, signaling increased airline competition for underserved markets.

Military Mar 28, 2026

Northrop Grumman Testbed With AIM-260-Like Nose Spotted Flying Alongside Raytheon’s VOODOO 1 at Eglin AFB

The two testbeds, known by their callsigns SCAN 06 and VOODOO 1, carried out concurrent operations in Eglin’s highly instrumented airspace. For three consecutive days, on Mar. 24, 25 and 26, two testbed aircraft, the Northrop Grumman CRJ-700 carrying the U.S. registration code N806X and typically...

Concurrent testing of next-generation air-to-air missile systems by competing defense contractors signals accelerated development timelines for advanced air combat capabilities.

Safety Mar 29, 2026

Surface Safety Spotlight – Los Angeles International Airport, CA (LAX) – From the Flight Deck

Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) is one of the nation’s busiest commercial airports. This short video highlights a runway incursion hazard where pilots exiting Runway 24R, particularly via...

Runway exit design and pilot procedural compliance remain critical safety factors at high-traffic airports where congestion increases incursion risk.

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Starfish Space finds a new partner for docking demonstration mission

Starfish Space has changed plans for a docking demonstration mission after its original partner backed out.

Starfish Space's ability to secure alternative partnership support suggests market confidence in orbital servicing technology despite setbacks in execution timelines.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 28, 2026

Germany Considering MQ-28A Ghost Bat CCA, Says Defense Minister

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius said the MQ-28 Ghost Bat is among the uncrewed aircraft being evaluated to support the Eurofighter Typhoon fighter fleet. Germany’s defense minister Boris Pistorius said during his visit to Australia on Mar. 27, 2026, that Berlin is considering Boeing Defence...

Germany's evaluation of the MQ-28 Ghost Bat signals NATO's shift toward integrated unmanned-manned teaming architectures to extend fighter fleet capabilities beyond traditional platforms.

See The Aviationist →

Military Mar 28, 2026

USMC F-35Cs and USS Tripoli ARG Arrive in the Middle East

A month after the start of the hostilities, the U.S. Marine Corps is increasing its presence in the Middle East with the arrival of a F-35C squadron and the USS Tripoli Amphibious Ready Group. Mar. 28, 2026, marks a month since the U.S. and Israel launched Operations Epic Fury and Roaring Lion,...

The expedited deployment of carrier-capable F-35Cs demonstrates the rapid mobilization capability of naval aviation forces in regional contingencies.

See The Aviationist →

Industry Mar 29, 2026

Towards Environmentally Sustainable Aviation: A Review on Operational Optimization

Growing air traffic is straining airports and airspace while raising environmental concerns, and demand is only expected to increase. This paper reviews how advanced technologies and methods can expand capacity and improve efficiency across airline, airport, and flight operations. It also highlights emerging trends and gaps for future research in sustainable aviation.

Operational optimization across all aviation domains offers immediate emissions reductions without waiting for next-generation aircraft, presenting a near-term sustainability advantage for airlines and airports.

See MDPI →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Robinson Unmanned

Robinson Unmanned (formerly Ascent AeroSystems) is the uncrewed aircraft systems (UAS) business unit of Robinson...

Robinson's transition from startup Ascent AeroSystems to a UAS division within an established helicopter manufacturer signals vertical integration consolidation in the commercial drone sector.

See sUAS News →

Drones Mar 28, 2026

Quantum Systems Strengthens Ukraine’s Air Defence Capabilities with 15,000 Interceptor Contract and WIY Drones Investment

In alignment with the defence priorities set out by the Ukrainian Government, Quantum Systems has...

German drone manufacturer's major Ukraine contract signals commercial UAS sector's expanded role in modern peer-conflict air defense beyond traditional surveillance.

Regulatory Mar 28, 2026

Singapore delays SAF levy rollout amid Middle East conflict

As the war in Iran continues to roil world oil markets, Singapore has delayed the rollout of its…...

Geopolitical oil market volatility is forcing regulators to postpone fuel-tax implementations, highlighting how external conflicts directly impact aviation's operating cost structures.

See AeroTime Hub →

Aerospace Mar 28, 2026

Artemis 2 astronauts arrive at KSC

The four Artemis 2 astronauts arrived in Florida March 27 for final preparations ahead of a launch still scheduled as soon as April 1.

Artemis 2's compressed pre-launch timeline demonstrates NASA's confidence in readiness after rigorous testing, setting a precedent for accelerated deep-space mission schedules.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

[Updated] Iranian Attack on Prince Sultan Air Base Reportedly Struck U.S. KC-135s, E-3 Sentry

Satellite images show signs of an attack at Prince Sultan Air Base, Saudi Arabia, with The Wall Street Journal now reporting that an Iranian missile damaged “several U.S. refueling aircraft.” Satellite images emerged on Mar. 27, 2026, appeared to show signs of damage on an apron used by U.S....

Damage to KC-135 tankers could significantly impact U.S. air operations' aerial refueling capacity in the region, complicating sustained combat missions.

See The Aviationist →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

I Am Artemis: Erik Richards

Listen to this audio excerpt from Erik Richards, Near Space Network Mission Manager: For Erik Richards, supporting NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission to the Moon and back is the culmination of a career spent helping spacecraft communicate with Earth. Like many kids who grew up at the height of the...

Deep Space Network expertise developed over decades is now critical infrastructure enabling NASA's return to lunar exploration, representing a strategic shift in how mission communication capabilities drive exploration architecture.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Selects Intuitive Machines to Deliver Artemis Science, Tech to Moon

NASA has awarded Intuitive Machines of Houston, $180.4 million to deliver NASA-funded science and technology to the lunar surface as part of the agency’s CLPS (Commercial Lunar Payload Services) initiative and Artemis program. This lunar delivery, which includes seven payloads — five of them NASA’s...

NASA's $180.4M investment in Intuitive Machines demonstrates the agency's reliance on commercial partners to reduce costs and accelerate lunar exploration infrastructure ahead of crewed Artemis missions.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA’s Environment and Energy “Blue Marble” Awards Categories

Category I: NASA Environmental Quality Award Recognizes excellence in environmental management and planning, including stewardship of natural and cultural resources. This category highlights achievements in compliance, conservation, remediation, communication, and environmental information...

NASA's environmental awards framework establishes formal recognition criteria for aerospace organizations integrating sustainability practices, creating measurable benchmarks for industry-wide environmental accountability.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

EASA warns non-airworthy stolen engine parts could re-enter the market

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency (EASA) is warning airlines, maintenance providers and parts buyers to be on…...

Stolen engine components from scrapped aircraft may be laundered back into supply chains, creating undetectable airworthiness risks that bypass standard certification processes.

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

Commercial Space Federation (CSF) Welcomes Two New Associate Members

March 27, 2026 – Washington, D.C.—The Commercial Space Federation (CSF) is pleased to welcome Astrolab and Zeno Power, two innovative companies advancing planetary mobility and reliable energy in extreme environments. […]...

The CSF's expansion to include lunar mobility and advanced power systems suppliers signals accelerating commercialization of deep-space infrastructure essential for sustained extraplanetary operations.

See SpaceNews →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

ESA to decide by June on Europe’s Gateway contributions

MUNICH — For more than a year, questions have swirled about Artemis changes and the Gateway’s role in U.S. lunar ambitions. Following NASA’s March 24 decision to halt work on Gateway, the lunar-orbiting station that had been intended to support astronauts before and after lunar surface missions,...

NASA's Gateway halt creates immediate budget and partnership uncertainty for ESA, forcing European space agencies to reassess lunar program commitments within three months.

See SpaceNews →

Military Mar 27, 2026

Gallery: Luke Days 2026

A glimpse back at last weekend's air show at Luke Air Force Base in Arizona.

Luke Air Force Base's recurring air show demonstrates the strategic importance of maintaining public engagement and recruitment visibility for military aviation operations.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR’s View of Mount Rainier

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount Rainier. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar enables all-weather Earth observation independent of cloud cover, expanding persistent monitoring capabilities for geological and environmental surveillance.

See NASA →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NISAR Views Mount St. Helens

Description This image captured by U.S.-Indian Earth satellite NISAR on Nov. 10, 2025, shows Washington’s Mount St. Helens. The image is cropped from a much larger swath spanning the Pacific Northwest on a cloudy day; NISAR’s L-band SAR instrument is able to peer through the clouds at the surface...

NISAR's L-band synthetic aperture radar demonstrates critical all-weather Earth observation capabilities for monitoring volcanic and geological hazards regardless of atmospheric conditions.

See NASA →

Safety Mar 27, 2026

NTSB Issues Preliminary Report on Hudson River Ditching

Investigators say an instructional flight ended after a loss of oil pressure and total engine failure.

Loss of oil pressure as a precursor to complete engine failure highlights the critical importance of real-time engine monitoring systems and pre-flight oil inspection protocols in preventing catastrophic in-flight emergencies.

See AVweb →

Aerospace Mar 27, 2026

NASA Tech and Science Bound for Low Earth Orbit on Commercial Launch

Technology and science demonstrations, supported by various NASA industry collaborations and agency developments, are set to launch to low Earth orbit aboard a SpaceX Falcon 9 rocket as part of the company’s Transporter-16 commercial rideshare mission. These demonstrations will test thermal...

NASA's use of commercial rideshare missions reduces launch costs for technology demonstrations while validating SpaceX's Falcon 9 as a reliable platform for diverse payload integration.

See NASA →
